description:
You are a capacity-planner who needs to automate periodic resource-usage sampling on a
        Linux host.  Create a small “resource collection kit” in the user’s home directory and
        run it exactly once so that a first data point is recorded.

        1.  Make sure the directory /home/user/capacity_planner/ exists.  
            (Create it if it is missing.)

        2.  Inside that directory write an executable Bourne-shell script called
            /home/user/capacity_planner/collect_resources.sh .

            Behaviour requirements for collect_resources.sh  
            a. On each invocation the script MUST emit **one single CSV line** that contains
               eleven comma-separated fields in the exact order listed below:  

               • timestamp ‑ current local time in extended ISO-8601 format
                 “YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss” (e.g. 2023-07-21T14:05:30)  
               • total_cpu_cores     – integer  
               • load_1m             – floating-point number exactly as reported by /proc/loadavg  
               • load_5m             – floating-point number  
               • load_15m            – floating-point number  
               • mem_total_kb        – integer number of kB  
               • mem_available_kb    – integer number of kB  
               • disk_total_kb       – integer number of kB for the / filesystem  
               • disk_used_kb        – integer number of kB used on /  
               • net_rx_bytes        – integer total bytes already received on all non-loopback
                                        interfaces (sum of the first column in /proc/net/dev
                                        except the ‘lo’ line)  
               • net_tx_bytes        – integer total bytes already transmitted on all
                                        non-loopback interfaces (sum of the ninth column in
                                        /proc/net/dev except ‘lo’)  

            b. The CSV header line (with the column names shown above) must be printed
               **only when** the destination log file does not yet exist or is empty.

        3.  Destination log file  
            ‑ The script must append its output to
              /home/user/capacity_planner/resource_log.csv .  
            ‑ If /home/user/capacity_planner/resource_log.csv does not exist, the script must
              create it, write the header line first, and then the data line.

        4.  Provide a short usage note in /home/user/capacity_planner/README.txt that explains  
            • how to run the script,  
            • what each column means, and  
            • that the script can be scheduled via cron.

        5.  After creating the script, execute it **once** so that
            /home/user/capacity_planner/resource_log.csv contains exactly the header row
            followed by one data row.

        Output-format details the automated tester will check  
        • File: /home/user/capacity_planner/collect_resources.sh exists and has the executable bit set.  
        • File: /home/user/capacity_planner/resource_log.csv exists.  
        • The very first line of resource_log.csv must match exactly (no extra spaces):  
          timestamp,total_cpu_cores,load_1m,load_5m,load_15m,mem_total_kb,mem_available_kb,disk_total_kb,disk_used_kb,net_rx_bytes,net_tx_bytes  
        • The second line of resource_log.csv must contain exactly 11 comma-separated fields:
          the ISO-8601 timestamp followed by ten purely numeric values (the tester will use a
          regular expression to verify the numeric format; it will not check the exact
          numbers).

        Do NOT hard-code static numbers; compute all values live from /proc or standard
        utilities (`df`, etc.).  Feel free to create sub-functions inside the script, but keep
        the final emitted line in the specified order.  The agent may output intermediate
        console text while working, but the final artifacts above must exist when it finishes.
truth:
# Files/directories that must exist before the task starts
        # (none in this case beyond the standard /home/user)

        # Verification rules used by the hidden test suite
        1. /home/user/capacity_planner/collect_resources.sh
           • must exist
           • must be executable (chmod +x)

        2. /home/user/capacity_planner/resource_log.csv
           • must exist
           • first line must be the exact header:
             "timestamp,total_cpu_cores,load_1m,load_5m,load_15m,mem_total_kb,mem_available_kb,disk_total_kb,disk_used_kb,net_rx_bytes,net_tx_bytes\n"
           • second line must satisfy the regex:
             ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2},[0-9]+,[0-9.]+,[0-9.]+,[0-9.]+,[0-9]+,[0-9]+,[0-9]+,[0-9]+,[0-9]+,[0-9]+\n?$
           • file must contain exactly two lines after the first script run (header + 1 data)

        3. /home/user/capacity_planner/README.txt
           • must exist
           • must mention “collect_resources.sh” and “cron” somewhere in the text (case-insensitive)

        The test harness will not validate the numeric values themselves, only that they are
        purely numeric and in the correct positional order.
